---
title: "Research"
---

## Work in progress

### Do Renewables Dampen Energy Price Shocks? Utility-Level Evidence from the Russia-Ukraine War

I investigate whether renewable energy penetration shields US counties from higher energy prices stemming from exogenous oil price shocks, using the Russia-Ukraine War as a natural experiment.

### The Political Economy of Federal-State Tax Conformity

### Autocratization and Judicial Capture

Do autocratic leaders systematically erode judicial independence, and through what channels? We study this question using a staggered difference-in-differences estimator robust to heterogeneous treatment effects, exploiting variation in the timing of democratic backsliding across 150 countries from 1950 to 2023. Our main specification finds that a democratic reversal reduces judicial constraints on the executive by 0.055 index points on impact, with effects accumulating over subsequent years. To sharpen identification, we introduce a new dataset — the first systematic coding of autocratic leader entry and constitutional authority acquisition for all post-war autocratization episodes — and show that effects are 40 percent larger when a leader gains constitutional authority to restructure institutions (−0.071) than at mere entry (−0.051). This sequencing is consistent with a two-stage theory of judicial capture in which entry precedes consolidation. Decomposing the mechanism, we find that autocratization simultaneously triggers judicial purges, court packing, attacks on the judiciary, executive non-compliance with court rulings, and erosion of high court independence — all six capture channels activating within the year of the shock. Results hold across ten independent treatment definitions spanning V-Dem polyarchy thresholds, DDCG democratic reversal events, REIGN leadership transitions, and FST populist leader codings, with flat pre-trends in all specifications.

---

## Theses

### A Crisis of Consensus: Austerity and the Rise of Political Polarization

*MRes thesis, Sciences Po Paris, May 2025. Supervisor: Dr. Moritz Schularick. Jury: Dr. Kevin O'Rourke. Grade: 16/20 (A).*

This study examines the political consequences of fiscal consolidations using a comprehensive dataset of austerity shocks and electoral outcomes across 15 advanced democracies from 1978 to 2016. It finds that austerity shocks significantly increase political polarization and support for non-mainstream parties over a four-year horizon, driven by austerity-induced economic insecurity. These results remain robust when controlling for financial crises and endogeneity. While the economic costs of austerity are larger during slumps than booms, the political effects are unexpectedly more pronounced when austerity is implemented during economic expansions. This divergence from the predictions of the economic insecurity channel suggests the presence of state-heterogeneous political responses to fiscal consolidation, offering new directions for future research.
